design_philosophy.core_loop: Assemble a party of four characters, explore overworld and dungeons, defeat enemies, collect marks and prayer cards, defeat Exodus
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Party composition and resource management against escalating dungeon difficulty; knowledge gaps (player cannot see beyond mountains/forests, creating exploration tension)
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Lead a customized party of adventurers on a quest to save the world from a supernatural threat; experience tactical party-based combat
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Party-based tactical combat in dungeons contrasts sharply with overworld movement as unified group; dungeon level-scaling independent of character level creates risk/reward for exploration; asymmetric class design forces meaningful party composition decisions

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High player agency: party composition (11 classes, 5 races, stat allocation) directly impacts survivability; dungeon exploration is optional and can be attempted at any level; enemies on overworld are visible and can be avoided; player can choose engagement moments

progression.upgrade_system: Character leveling to maximum 25; stat growth tied to race selection; equipment discovery in dungeons and shops; spell acquisition by class
progression.unlock_mechanism: Four marks obtained from four dungeons unlock progression gates; prayer cards required for final confrontation; pirate ship defeat unlocks boat travel to island of Ambrosia; level thresholds unlock pirate ship encounters
replayability_hooks: 11 character classes with distinct ability profiles enable different party compositions
5 races with stat limitation variations affect character ceiling
Stat allocation at creation creates build diversity
Overworld contains hidden treasures and secret paths requiring exploration
Multiple travel modes (foot, horseback, boat) with different strategic implications

world_building_techniques: NPC dialogue reveals lore and quest objectives
Party must locate specific townspeople and informants for guidance
Dungeon integration into plot (marks as story-gated progression)
World of Sosaria threatened by Exodus (spawn of previous antagonists Mondain and Minax)
Overworld exploration reveals hidden areas containing story elements and treasure

boss_design.philosophy: Exodus as climactic antagonist requiring narrative buildup and multi-stage preparation (acquiring marks and prayer cards); represents culmination of 'Age of Darkness' trilogy narrative
notable_bosses: Exodus (final antagonist; requires all four marks and prayer cards to defeat)
Lord British (unkillable in normal circumstances; serves as immovable NPC obstacle; can be temporarily killed via cannon fire but respawns)

faction_indicators: Lord British as authority figure; guards enforce law (8-guard parties pursue criminals)
Ability to bribe guards suggests corruption/coercion as faction mechanic
danger_signals: Theft from NPCs triggers guard pursuit
Fighting townspeople escalates to guard encounter (very difficult 8-guard parties)
Dungeon depth scaling creates level-gating and resource depletion risk
Random dungeon encounters vs. visible overworld threats create asymmetric danger perception
environmental_storytelling: Mountains, forests, and walls hide secrets, creating discovery-driven exploration
Dungeon architecture (8 levels, increasing difficulty) signals danger escalation by depth
Trapped chests and fountain variety (healing/curing/poison) create environment-based hazards
Pirate ship encounters on overworld signal progression milestone

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Ultima: Exodus (NES)
